text: Here we present our procedure for a transperineal approach for secondary rectal cancer following prior radiotherapy for prostate cancer. The patient was a ninety-year-old man with low rectal cancer located just above the anal verge. The tumor was located mainly on the right side. The most important problem with this patient was that he had received radiation therapy with a total dose of seventy-six gray for prostate cancer eleven years earlier and the rectal cancer occurred within the irradiated area.

The laparoscopic view shows the operative field where the left-sided colon is mobilized and the inferior mesenteric artery is ligated. During posterior dissection, the surgeon pushes up the posterior wall of the rectum to extend the fibrous tissues. On the left side, triangulation using both hands of the assistant and the surgeon's left hand extends the fibrous tissues between the mesorectum and surrounding tissues. Mobilization of the mesorectum is challenging due to increased pelvic fibrosis from previous radiation therapy.

The surgeon makes a skin incision around the tightly closed anus and connects a gel point mini. The video shows the direction of the operative field relative to the anus in the lower left corner. Fat tissues in the ischioanal fossa are divided, exposing the levator ani muscle, which is then divided from the posterior side. Posterior presacral dissection is performed, with the posterior side of the rectum being pushed up to identify and divide the fibrous connective tissues covering the mesorectum.

In the upper right corner of the video, the abdominal view shows the posterior dissection connecting with the laparoscopic dissection layer. The levator ani muscle is divided bilaterally from the posterior side, and the dissection is extended to the lateral side.

During the lateral dissection, the surgeon identifies the neurovascular bundle as a landmark. The perineal dissection progresses with focus on the challenging anterior dissection, which is performed just behind the transverse perineal muscle.

The surgeon identifies the arms of the puborectalis sling at the one and eleven o'clock positions. The levator ani muscle in this region shows visible degenerative changes due to previous radiotherapy.

On the anterior side, the surgeon encounters severe fibrous changes in the connective tissues due to prior radiotherapy, making it difficult to identify a clear dissection layer. The transverse perineal muscle is visible amidst the dense fibrous tissue.

The surgeon identifies the neurovascular bundle as a key landmark for determining the dissection line. Dissection is performed near the neurovascular bundle, causing minor bleeding, which prompts adjustments to the dissection line based on visible bleeding points.

The abdominal view is displayed in the upper left corner as the surgeon carefully dissects to avoid injuring the urethra. Laparoscopic assistance aids in determining the dissection line during lateral anterior dissection. The perineal body is divided at the inferior border of the prostate. Rectal mobilization is completed, and the resected specimen is extracted through the anus. The transperineal view reveals the pelvic splanchnic nerve, neurovascular bundle, and the cut end of the levator ani muscle.

The video displays the perineal body, neurovascular bundle, prostate, and transverse perineal muscle. The anterior dissection plane of the rectal specimen shows increased fibrosis among smooth muscle cells, with collagen fibers stained light blue using Elastica Masson staining.
